headline: Deadly Tornado Strikes Baker, 1962
body: The wind, a beast unleashed, tore through Baker on April 11, 1962. It was not a gentle breeze, nor a mere squall, but a vortex of raw, untamed power, a funnel of destruction that descended upon the unsuspecting plains. Two souls, caught in its merciless grip, were snatched from life, their existence snuffed out in an instant. The air, thick with the scent of pulverized earth and splintered wood, bore witness to the storm's fury. Homes, once sturdy bastions against the elements, were ripped asunder, their timbers scattered like kindling. Infrastructure, the very sinews of the town, buckled and broke under the immense pressure. The roar was deafening, a primal scream that echoed the terror in the hearts of those who sought shelter. When the beast finally retreated, leaving behind a trail of devastation, the silence that followed was heavy, punctuated only by the cries of the injured and the stunned whispers of the survivors. The landscape was irrevocably altered, a testament to nature's indifferent might. One resident, surveying the wreckage, was heard to mutter, "It came like a thief in the night, and left us with naught but ruin and the memory of its terrible breath." The struggle for survival, for rebuilding, had just begun in the ravaged heart of Fallon County, a stark reminder of man's fragile hold against the wild, untamed forces of the Montana sky.
